narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which evaluates the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated a 51% probability that you would not pass. This prediction tips just over the threshold we use for admission decisions, leading to the rejection as a way to prioritize applicants with stronger projected outcomes. The model's assessment was most heavily shaped by three key elements in your profile: your LSAT score, family income bracket, and first-year grade decile. Above all, your LSAT score of 23 stood out as the strongest driver pushing the prediction toward failure—it's notably lower than the average of about 37 for students who passed the bar, signaling to the model a potential challenge in handling the exam's demands. In contrast, your lower-middle family income bracket worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success since it aligns with backgrounds that have succeeded despite not being the highest earners. Similarly, your fourth-decile standing in first-year grades provided a positive nudge, as it's closer to the stronger performance seen in passers compared to even lower ranks. Your third-year decile of 3 and undergraduate GPA of 2.6, while below the averages for bar passers (around 5.9 and 3.24 respectively), played lesser roles here, as did your choice to attend part-time, which is less common among successful students. Overall, your profile compares to admitted students by lagging most in entrance exam strength and early academic deciles, though your income and initial grades offer some counterbalance not always seen in similar cases. This isn't a judgment on your potential or hard work—many factors like resilience and study habits aren't captured here. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score significantly, as it's the top lever for shifting predictions, and perhaps seek targeted prep for bar-like reasoning skills. Strengthening your academic deciles through additional coursework or a post-bac program could also help rebuild your profile. We encourage reapplying after addressing these areas, as improvements in even one or two can dramatically change the outlook.
